proposition: To prevail on an ineffective assistance of counsel claim, a defendant must satisfy a two-prong test of deficient performance and prejudice.
brief_sentence: To prevail on a claim of ineffective assistance of counsel, a defendant must satisfy the two-prong test set forth in Strickland v. Washington, 466 U.S. 668 (1984), adopted by Georgia courts in Reynolds v. State, 306 Ga. 630 (2019).
brief_block: To prevail on a claim of ineffective assistance of counsel, a defendant must satisfy the two-prong test set forth in Strickland v. Washington, 466 U.S. 668 (1984) [...].
opinion_block: First, the defendant must show that counsel's performance was deficient. [...] Second, the defendant must show that the deficient performance prejudiced the defense. [...] Unless a defendant makes both showings, it cannot be said that the conviction or death sentence resulted from a breakdown in the adversary process that renders the result unreliable.
finding_analysis: Strickland squarely supports this proposition. The Court holds that an ineffective-assistance claim has two components -- deficient performance and prejudice -- and that the defendant must make both showings, which is exactly the two-prong test the brief describes.

proposition: Georgia courts adopted the Strickland two-prong test for ineffective assistance of counsel claims.
diagnostics: Party mismatch: found "State v. Mondor" shares only one party with cited "Reynolds v. State"; Name mismatch: cited "Reynolds v. State" vs found "State v. Mondor"; Citation mismatch: cited 306 Ga. 630 but CourtListener has 830 S.E.2d 206 306 Ga. 338
brief_sentence: To prevail on a claim of ineffective assistance of counsel, a defendant must satisfy the two-prong test set forth in Strickland v. Washington, 466 U.S. 668 (1984), adopted by Georgia courts in Reynolds v. State, 306 Ga. 630 (2019).
brief_block: To prevail on a claim of ineffective assistance of counsel, a defendant must satisfy the two-prong test set forth in Strickland v. Washington, 466 U.S. 668 (1984), adopted by Georgia courts in Reynolds v. State, 306 Ga. 630 (2019).
finding_analysis: The brief cites Reynolds v. State, 306 Ga. 630 (2019), but the opinion on file is Black v. State, 264 Ga. 550 (1994) -- a different case. The proposition cannot be grounded in the cited authority because the citation resolves to a different opinion. As an aside, the Black opinion does apply the Strickland two-prong framework, requiring a defendant to show that "counsel's performance was deficient" and "that the deficient performance prejudiced the defense," so the underlying legal principle is sound; but Black is not the case the brief invokes for the proposition that Georgia courts adopted Strickland, and nothing in this opinion establishes that Reynolds did so.

proposition: A valid citizen's arrest must occur immediately and without delay.
brief_sentence: Additionally, Georgia courts have made it clear that a valid citizen's arrest must occur immediately and without delay. (Harbuck v. State, 288 Ga. 768 (2011)).

proposition: The failure to request a jury instruction not supported by the evidence does not constitute deficient performance.
brief_sentence: Georgia law is clear that the failure to request a jury instruction not supported by the evidence does not constitute deficient performance. (Reed v. State, 291 Ga. 10 (2012); Davis v. State, 285 Ga. 343 (2009).)
brief_block: Georgia law is clear that the failure to request a jury instruction not supported by the evidence does not constitute deficient performance. (Reed v. State, 291 Ga. 10 (2012); Davis v. State, 285 Ga. 343 (2009).)
finding_analysis: Reed v. State is a Georgia Supreme Court murder case addressing sufficiency of the evidence, sufficiency of the indictment, the standard of review for similar transaction evidence, and ineffective assistance of counsel. It does not address the failure to request a jury instruction. The opinion's ineffective-assistance discussion under Strickland reaches only two grounds -- counsel's failure to challenge the indictment by special demurrer (Division 4(a)) and counsel's failure to object and move for mistrial in response to improper closing arguments about future dangerousness (Division 4(b)). Neither jury instructions in general nor the specific principle that failing to request an instruction unsupported by the evidence is not deficient performance appears anywhere in the opinion. The brief attributes a jury-instruction rule to Reed that the case does not contain.

finding_analysis: The brief cites Davis v. State, 285 Ga. 343 (2009), but the opinion provided resolves to a different decision: Davis v. State, 269 Ga. 276 (1998), a Benham, C.J. opinion affirming a felony-murder conviction (stabbing death of Danny Reid), with page markers running *277 through *281 and a crime date of 1993. The 285 Ga. 343 (2009) decision is not the document supplied. Because the cited case is not before me, I cannot ground a judgment on whether it supports the deficient-performance proposition. The 1998 opinion in hand does discuss the refusal to give a requested accident charge for lack of supporting evidence (Division 3), but it is a trial-court-error analysis, not an ineffective-assistance/deficient-performance holding, and in any event it is the wrong case.

proposition: Tactical and strategic decisions do not constitute ineffective assistance unless no competent attorney would have made the same choice.
brief_sentence: Tactical and strategic decisions do not constitute ineffective assistance unless no competent attorney would have made the same choice. (Head v. Ferrell, 274 Ga. 399 (2001); Rumph v. State, 307 Ga. 477 (2019)).
brief_block: Tactical and strategic decisions do not constitute ineffective assistance unless no competent attorney would have made the same choice. (Head v. Ferrell, 274 Ga. 399 (2001); Rumph v. State, 307 Ga. 477 (2019)).
opinion_block: [C]ounsel on appeal is "strongly presumed to have rendered adequate assistance and made all significant decisions in the exercise of reasonable professional judgment." . . . Because counsel's performance is considered in light of the circumstances surrounding the representation, reference to hindsight is inappropriate in judging counsel's performance.
finding_analysis: Head v. Ferrell applies the Strickland framework and gives strong deference to counsel's strategic decisions, holding that counsel is strongly presumed to have exercised reasonable professional judgment and that hindsight is inappropriate. That general principle is consistent with the brief's point. But the opinion never articulates the specific standard the brief attributes to it -- that a tactical decision constitutes ineffective assistance only when "no competent attorney would have made the same choice." The case states the deferential presumption and rejects the ineffectiveness claims on the facts; it does not announce the categorical "no competent attorney" test. The proposition is therefore an overstatement of what this opinion actually holds, even though it is in the same doctrinal territory.

finding_analysis: State v. Rumph is a criminal procedure case about whether Miranda warnings were required for a defendant's pre-arrest statements. It does not address ineffective assistance of counsel or the deference owed to defense counsel's tactical and strategic decisions. The opinion's sole holding is that, because a reasonable person in Rumph's situation would not have perceived that he was in custody during his first two interviews, Miranda warnings were not necessary, and the trial court erred in suppressing the statements. Nothing in the opinion discusses Strickland, ineffective assistance, or the "no competent attorney" standard the brief attributes to the case.

proposition: Justification is an affirmative defense that does not require a charge on citizen's arrest unless the facts specifically support that additional theory.
brief_sentence: Justification is an affirmative defense that includes the lawful use of force in defense of self, others, or property—and does not require a charge on citizen's arrest unless the facts specifically support that additional theory. (See, e.g., Manzano v. State, 282 Ga. 557 (2007)).
brief_block: Justification is an affirmative defense that includes the lawful use of force in defense of self, others, or property—and does not require a charge on citizen's arrest unless the facts specifically support that additional theory. (See, e.g., Manzano v. State, 282 Ga. 557 (2007)).
finding_analysis: Manzano v. State is a felony-murder case about whether the trial court erred in refusing to charge the jury on involuntary manslaughter (with pointing a pistol or reckless conduct as the predicate misdemeanor) and whether the State could ask the testifying defendant if other witnesses were lying. It does not address justification, affirmative defenses, the lawful use of force in defense of self, others, or property, or citizen's arrest. The words "justification," "affirmative defense," and "citizen's arrest" do not appear anywhere in the opinion, so the case cannot stand for the proposition asserted.

Page 559 of the opinion (the pincite given) concerns the rule, drawn from Rhodes v. The brief attributes a fabricated rule of law to a case that says nothing about when a justification charge is proper.

proposition: The Georgia Supreme Court rejected the need for a separate citizen's arrest instruction where the defendant claimed he was trying to lawfully detain someone committing a crime, holding the charge on justification was sufficient.
brief_sentence: In Manzano, the Georgia Supreme Court rejected the need for a separate citizen's arrest instruction where the defendant claimed he was trying to lawfully detain someone committing a crime. The Court held that the trial court's charge on justification was sufficient and appropriate.
brief_block: In Manzano, the Georgia Supreme Court rejected the need for a separate citizen's arrest instruction where the defendant claimed he was trying to lawfully detain someone committing a crime. The Court held that the trial court's charge on justification was sufficient and appropriate.
finding_analysis: Manzano v. State is a felony-murder case in which the defendant claimed his wife was shot inadvertently during horseplay with a pistol he believed was unloaded; the Court reversed the conviction because the trial court wrongly refused requested charges on involuntary manslaughter. It does not address citizen's arrest, justification, or lawfully detaining someone committing a crime. The opinion contains no holding rejecting a citizen's arrest instruction and no holding that a justification charge was sufficient — those concepts appear nowhere in the text. The brief invents a holding the case does not contain.

proposition: Plain error requires a clear legal mistake that affects substantial rights and the fairness of proceedings.
brief_sentence: Because the evidence did not support a separate charge on citizen's arrest, and the justification charge already covered the legal defense raised, the trial court did not err—plainly or otherwise. See State v. Kelly, 290 Ga. 29, 33 (2011) (plain error requires a clear legal mistake that affects substantial rights and the fairness of proceedings).
brief_block: Because the evidence did not support a separate charge on citizen's arrest, and the justification charge already covered the legal defense raised, the trial court did not err—plainly or otherwise. See State v. Kelly, 290 Ga. 29, 33 (2011) (plain error requires a clear legal mistake that affects substantial rights and the fairness of proceedings).
opinion_block: First, there must be an error or defect — some sort of “[d]eviation from a legal rule” — that has not been intentionally relinquished or abandoned... Second, the legal error must be clear or obvious, rather than subject to reasonable dispute. Third, the error must have affected the appellant’s substantial rights... Fourth and finally... discretion which ought to be exercised only if the error “‘seriously affect[s] the fairness, integrity or public reputation of judicial proceedings.’”
finding_analysis: Kelly adopts the federal four-prong plain error standard at the cited page (290 Ga. at 33), requiring a clear or obvious legal error that affected the appellant's substantial rights and, in the court's discretion, seriously affects the fairness, integrity, or public reputation of the proceedings. The brief's parenthetical — a clear legal mistake affecting substantial rights and the fairness of proceedings — accurately compresses that standard and is directly supported by the opinion.

proposition: A defendant claiming ineffective assistance must demonstrate deficient performance and resulting prejudice, and failure to raise a speculative or unproven defense does not constitute ineffective assistance.
cited_for: Failure to raise a speculative or unproven defense does not constitute ineffective assistance.
brief_sentence: Failure to raise a speculative or unproven defense does not constitute ineffective assistance. See Chapman v. State, 273 Ga. 348 (2001).
brief_block: Failure to raise a speculative or unproven defense does not constitute ineffective assistance. See Chapman v. State, 273 Ga. 348 (2001).
opinion_block: Trial counsel's "pursuit of a guilty but mentally ill verdict conformed to a reasonable pattern of trial strategy and advocacy by one familiar with the intricacies of a death penalty case" and "was integral to a trial scheme to avoid a death sentence where evidence of guilt of [the] murder[s] was overwhelming and legitimate factual defenses were non-existent."
finding_analysis:
Chapman v. State is the correct case and is squarely an ineffective-assistance-of-counsel decision applying Strickland's deficient-performance-and-prejudice standard, so the citation is on-topic. The court did hold that counsel's decision not to present the defendant's proposed version of events was not deficient performance, which is broadly consistent with the brief's point that declining to raise a defense need not be ineffective assistance.

The gap is in characterization. The opinion's rationale was not that the rejected defense was merely "speculative or unproven." Counsel knew the defendant's proposed account was untrue, and the court grounded its holding on the facts that the evidence of guilt was overwhelming, "legitimate factual defenses were non-existent," and counsel could not assist in presenting false evidence. The brief recasts a known-false-defense / no-legitimate-defense holding as a rule about "speculative or unproven" defenses, an overstatement and reframing of what the case actually decided. Topically related and reasonably extendable, but not a direct match for the proposition as phrased.

proposition: Strategic decisions not to present speculative evidence do not amount to ineffective assistance.
brief_sentence: Georgia courts have consistently held that strategic decisions not to present speculative evidence do not amount to ineffective assistance. See Black v. State, 264 Ga. 550 (1994); Head v. Carr, 273 Ga. 613 (2001).
brief_block: Georgia courts have consistently held that strategic decisions not to present speculative evidence do not amount to ineffective assistance. See Black v. State, 264 Ga. 550 (1994) [...].
opinion_block: The attorney's decision to pursue the defense of insanity instead of self-defense was part of legitimate trial strategy and falls within the range of reasonable professional assistance.
finding_analysis:
Black v. State supports the general principle that reasonable strategic decisions by trial counsel do not amount to ineffective assistance. The court held that counsel's choice to pursue an insanity defense over self-defense "was part of legitimate trial strategy and falls within the range of reasonable professional assistance," and quoted with approval the rule that difficult tactical decisions "with which appellant and his present counsel now disagree" do not render representation ineffective.

The fit is partial, however, because the brief frames the principle specifically around "strategic decisions not to present speculative evidence." Black does not address speculative evidence as such; its strategic-decision holding concerns the choice between two affirmative defenses (insanity vs. self-defense). The case reasonably extends to the broader proposition, but the brief's narrower "speculative evidence" formulation is not the precise holding of this opinion.

proposition: Strategic decisions not to present speculative evidence do not amount to ineffective assistance.
brief_sentence: Georgia courts have consistently held that strategic decisions not to present speculative evidence do not amount to ineffective assistance. See Black v. State, 264 Ga. 550 (1994); Head v. Carr, 273 Ga. 613 (2001).
brief_block: Georgia courts have consistently held that strategic decisions not to present speculative evidence do not amount to ineffective assistance. See Black v. State, 264 Ga. 550 (1994); Head v. Carr, 273 Ga. 613 (2001).
opinion_block: courts must be highly deferential to trial counsel's reasonable strategic decisions
finding_analysis: Head v. Carr is a death-penalty habeas decision in which the Georgia Supreme Court reverses the habeas court's grant of ineffective-assistance relief, repeatedly holding that trial counsel's reasonable strategic decisions about which witnesses and evidence to present do not amount to deficient performance. The opinion supports the proposition: it credits counsel's strategic reasons for declining to call additional family members ("a valid strategic reason for limiting the use of these witnesses") and for not presenting other peripheral or unreliable evidence, instructing that "courts must be highly deferential to trial counsel's reasonable strategic decisions." The opinion also uses the "speculative" framing in the closely related prejudice context, holding that the claimed harm from counsel's omission was "too speculative to support a finding of ineffective assistance of counsel." The brief's phrasing ("strategic decisions not to present speculative evidence") is a slight gloss on holdings that more precisely concern declining to present unreliable or marginal evidence, but the citation accurately captures the case's core principle of deference to counsel's strategic presentation choices.

proposition: Strategic choices focusing on legally relevant issues and avoiding speculative claims are entitled to deference.
diagnostics: The citation resolved in CourtListener's index, but no case name was available to compare (no name parsed from the citation). The cited case is unconfirmed — treat as partial verification.; citation resolved; no case name available to compare
brief_sentence: This strategy focused the jury on legally relevant issues and avoided speculative claims. Such strategic choices are entitled to deference. Strickland, 466 U.S. at 689.
brief_block: This strategy focused the jury on legally relevant issues and avoided speculative claims. Such strategic choices are entitled to deference. Strickland, 466 U.S. at 689.
opinion_block: Because of the difficulties inherent in making the evaluation, a court must indulge a strong presumption that counsel's conduct falls within the wide range of reasonable professional assistance; that is, the defendant must overcome the presumption that, under the circumstances, the challenged action "might be considered sound trial strategy."
finding_analysis: The cited principle -- that counsel's strategic choices are entitled to deference -- is directly supported at page 689, where the Court instructs that judicial scrutiny of counsel's performance must be highly deferential and that strategic decisions carry a strong presumption of reasonableness. The brief's gloss about focusing on "legally relevant issues" and avoiding "speculative claims" is the brief's application of the deference rule to its facts rather than language from the opinion, but the deference principle itself is accurately attributed.

proposition: A defendant claiming ineffective assistance must establish deficient performance and prejudice, and strategic decisions, even if ultimately unsuccessful, are entitled to strong deference.
brief_sentence: As stated, under Strickland, 466 U.S. 668 (1984), and adopted by Georgia courts in Reynolds, 306 Ga. 630 (2019), a defendant claiming ineffective assistance must establish both (1) that trial counsel's performance was deficient, and (2) that the deficient performance prejudiced the defense.
brief_block: As stated, under Strickland, 466 U.S. 668 (1984) [...] a defendant claiming ineffective assistance must establish both (1) that trial counsel's performance was deficient, and (2) that the deficient performance prejudiced the defense.
opinion_block: Counsel's strategy choice was well within the range of professionally reasonable judgments [...] there can be little question, even without application of the presumption of adequate performance, that trial counsel's defense, though unsuccessful, was the result of reasonable professional judgment.
finding_analysis: Both halves of the proposition are supported. The Court establishes the two-prong test requiring a showing of deficient performance and prejudice, and it holds that counsel's conduct is reviewed under a strong presumption of reasonableness -- applying that deference here to conclude counsel's strategy, "though unsuccessful, was the result of reasonable professional judgment."

proposition: Georgia courts adopted the Strickland two-prong test for ineffective assistance of counsel claims.
brief_sentence: As stated, under Strickland, 466 U.S. 668 (1984), and adopted by Georgia courts in Reynolds, 306 Ga. 630 (2019), a defendant claiming ineffective assistance must establish both (1) that trial counsel's performance was deficient, and (2) that the deficient performance prejudiced the defense.

proposition: Strategic decisions, even if ultimately unsuccessful, are entitled to strong deference.
brief_sentence: Strategic decisions, even if ultimately unsuccessful, are entitled to strong deference. (Head v. Ferrell, 274 Ga. 399 (2001); Rumph v. State, 307 Ga. 477 (2019).)
brief_block: Strategic decisions, even if ultimately unsuccessful, are entitled to strong deference. (Head v. Ferrell, 274 Ga. 399 (2001); Rumph v. State, 307 Ga. 477 (2019).)
finding_analysis: Head v. Ferrell directly supports this proposition. The opinion holds that counsel is "strongly presumed to have rendered adequate assistance and made all significant decisions in the exercise of reasonable professional judgment" and that "reference to hindsight is inappropriate in judging counsel's performance." In applying that standard, the court repeatedly defers to trial counsel's strategic choices -- for example, the chosen "residual doubt" mitigation theory and the decision not to raise a Batson challenge -- treating them as reasonable professional judgments notwithstanding that the defense was unsuccessful. That is precisely the strong deference to (even unsuccessful) strategic decisions the brief asserts.

It says nothing about strategic decisions, deference to counsel, or the consequences of unsuccessful strategy, so it cannot support the proposition for which the brief cites it.

proposition: Where neither instruction was supported by the evidence, trial counsel's strategic decision not to request them was objectively reasonable, and counsel is not ineffective for declining to pursue a meritless or inapplicable defense.
brief_sentence: Because neither instruction was supported by the evidence, trial counsel's strategic decision not to request them was objectively reasonable. See Mahan v. State, 282 Ga. App. 201, 204 (2006). Counsel is not ineffective for declining to pursue a meritless or inapplicable defense.
brief_block: Because neither instruction was supported by the evidence, trial counsel's strategic decision not to request them was objectively reasonable. See Mahan v. State, 282 Ga. App. 201, 204 (2006). Counsel is not ineffective for declining to pursue a meritless or inapplicable defense.
opinion_block: Trial counsel's decisions as to tactics and strategy, including the scope of cross-examination, generally do not amount to ineffective assistance of counsel. [...] Because trial counsel is not required to make a futile objection, Mahan has not established ineffective assistance of counsel.
finding_analysis:
Mahan does not address jury instructions or charges at all. The opinion's ineffective-assistance analysis concerns trial counsel's investigation of the medical evidence, the scope of cross-examination of Dr. Dunton, and the failure to object to prior-difficulty evidence -- not a decision to forgo requesting jury instructions unsupported by the evidence.

The brief's general principle that counsel is not ineffective for declining a meritless or inapplicable defense draws support by analogy from two strands of the opinion: that strategic and tactical decisions generally do not amount to ineffective assistance, and that counsel is not required to make a futile objection. But the specific framing tied to unrequested, evidence-unsupported instructions is the brief's own extension; the case supports the broad proposition only obliquely and does not decide the instruction-specific point.

proposition: The absence of unsupported charges did not affect the outcome of the trial, and the defendant bears the burden of proving both deficient performance and prejudice.
brief_sentence: Moreover, the absence of such unsupported charges did not affect the outcome of the trial. See Strickland v. Washington, 466 U.S. 668 (1984). Also, defendant bears the burden of proving both deficient performance and prejudice under Strickland. Neither burden is met here.
brief_block: Moreover, the absence of such unsupported charges did not affect the outcome of the trial. See Strickland v. Washington, 466 U.S. 668 (1984). Also, defendant bears the burden of proving both deficient performance and prejudice under Strickland. Neither burden is met here.
opinion_block: Conflict of interest claims aside, actual ineffectiveness claims alleging a deficiency in attorney performance are subject to a general requirement that the defendant affirmatively prove prejudice. [...] Failure to make the required showing of either deficient performance or sufficient prejudice defeats the ineffectiveness claim.
finding_analysis: The cited-for legal principle -- that the defendant bears the burden of proving both deficient performance and prejudice -- is directly supported. The Court requires the defendant to affirmatively prove prejudice and holds that failure to show either deficient performance or sufficient prejudice defeats the claim. The brief's "did not affect the outcome" assertion mirrors the opinion's prejudice standard, which asks whether there is a reasonable probability the result would have been different but for counsel's errors.

brief_block: The decision whether to pursue a defense theory or request a specific jury charge is “a question of trial strategy” that will not be second-guessed unless it is “so patently unreasonable that no competent attorney would have chosen it.” Smith v. State, 314 Ga. App. 583, 588 (2012).
opinion_block: Generally, decisions about which defense theories to pursue and which jury charges to request are strategic and “provide no grounds for reversal unless such tactical decisions are so patently unreasonable that no competent attorney would have chosen them.”
finding_analysis:
The cited case supports the underlying proposition. At page 588 the opinion holds that “decisions about which defense theories to pursue and which jury charges to request are strategic” and provide no grounds for reversal “unless such tactical decisions are so patently unreasonable that no competent attorney would have chosen them.” That is exactly the trial-strategy/deference principle the brief invokes.

The quotation marks, however, are not accurate. “A question of trial strategy” does not appear in the opinion; the court says the decisions “are strategic.” And the brief’s second quotation ends “...chosen it,” whereas the opinion reads “...chosen them,” and the quoted phrase is part of the longer clause “provide no grounds for reversal unless such tactical decisions are so patently unreasonable....” The substance is sound, but counsel has presented reworded language as a verbatim quote and should conform the quotation to the opinion’s actual words.

brief_block: The terms "in his presence" and "within his immediate knowledge" are synonymous, meaning that a crime is considered to be committed in a citizen's presence if they acquire knowledge of its commission through the exercise of any of their senses. Johnson v. Jackson, 140 Ga. App. 252 (1976), Merneigh v. State, 242 Ga. App. 735 (2000).
opinion_block: OCGA § 17-4-60 provides, in pertinent part, that a private citizen may arrest an offender “if the offense is committed in his presence or within his immediate knowledge.” ... The term “within his immediate knowledge” enables a private citizen to use any of his senses to obtain knowledge that an offense is being committed.
finding_analysis:
Merneigh supports the "any of their senses" half of the proposition: the opinion holds that the statutory term "within his immediate knowledge" enables a private citizen to use any of his senses to obtain knowledge that an offense is being committed, so a citizen need not actually be present.

But the opinion does not treat "in his presence" and "within his immediate knowledge" as synonymous; it does the opposite. The court stresses that OCGA § 17-4-60 uses the disjunctive "or" (emphasis supplied in the opinion) and applies the two phrases to different actors -- the food manager's authority rested on presence, the store manager's on immediate knowledge. Treating the two terms as synonymous overstates and mischaracterizes the holding, which keeps them as distinct alternatives. The citation is therefore only a partial fit.

proposition: For misdemeanors, the offense must occur in the citizen's presence or immediate knowledge, while for felonies, a citizen may also arrest upon reasonable and probable grounds of suspicion if the offender is escaping or attempting to escape.
brief_sentence: For misdemeanors, the offense must occur in the citizen's presence or immediate knowledge, while for felonies, a citizen may also arrest upon reasonable and probable grounds of suspicion if the offender is escaping or attempting to escape. Johnson v. Jackson, 140 Ga. App. 252, Delegal v. State, 109 Ga. 518, 521 (1900), Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56 (1995).
brief_block: For misdemeanors, the offense must occur in the citizen's presence or immediate knowledge, while for felonies, a citizen may also arrest upon reasonable and probable grounds of suspicion if the offender is escaping or attempting to escape. Johnson v. Jackson, 140 Ga. App. 252 [...].
opinion_block: [A] private person may arrest an offender, if the offense is committed in his presence or within his immediate knowledge; and if the offense is a felony and the offender is escaping, or attempting to escape, a private person may arrest him upon reasonable and probable grounds of suspicion.
finding_analysis: Johnson v. Jackson supports this proposition. At *257 the opinion quotes Code Sec. 27-211, which draws exactly the distinction the brief states: an offense must be committed in the citizen's presence or within his immediate knowledge to support an arrest, and where the offense is a felony and the offender is escaping or attempting to escape, the citizen may arrest upon reasonable and probable grounds of suspicion.

proposition: For misdemeanors, the offense must occur in the citizen's presence or immediate knowledge, while for felonies, a citizen may also arrest upon reasonable and probable grounds of suspicion if the offender is escaping or attempting to escape.
brief_sentence: For misdemeanors, the offense must occur in the citizen's presence or immediate knowledge, while for felonies, a citizen may also arrest upon reasonable and probable grounds of suspicion if the offender is escaping or attempting to escape. Johnson v. Jackson, 140 Ga. App. 252, Delegal v. State, 109 Ga. 518, 521 (1900), Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56 (1995).
brief_block: For misdemeanors, the offense must occur in the citizen's presence or immediate knowledge, while for felonies, a citizen may also arrest upon reasonable and probable grounds of suspicion if the offender is escaping or attempting to escape. Johnson v. Jackson, 140 Ga. App. 252, Delegal v. State, 109 Ga. 518, 521 (1900), Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56 (1995).
opinion_block: It is a well-settled principle of the law of this State, and, as far as we are advised, of all other jurisdictions, that a private individual can not make an arrest for a misdemeanor, unless the offense is committed in his presence or within his immediate knowledge. Our Penal Code, § 900, declares that “A private person may arrest an offender if the offense is committed in his presence or within his immediate knowledge; and if the offense is a felony, and the offender is escaping, or attempting to escape, a private person may arrest him upon reasonable and probable grounds of suspicion.”
finding_analysis: Delegal v. State directly supports the proposition. The opinion, quoting Penal Code § 900, states that a private person may arrest for any offense committed in his presence or within his immediate knowledge, and that for a felony where the offender is escaping or attempting to escape, a private person may arrest upon reasonable and probable grounds of suspicion. The court further articulates the misdemeanor limitation as a well-settled principle: a private individual cannot arrest for a misdemeanor unless the offense was committed in his presence or within his immediate knowledge. The brief's statement of the misdemeanor-versus-felony distinction tracks the cited case accurately.

The brief grafts a misdemeanor rule onto a passage that does not contain one, so the citation only partially supports the stated proposition.

opinion_block: deadly force in effecting an arrest is limited to self-defense or to a situation in which it is necessary to prevent a forcible felony.
finding_analysis: Prayor supports the 'prevent a forcible felony' branch and, broadly, the 'self-defense' branch of this proposition. But the opinion states the rule simply as 'self-defense' and does not articulate the elaborated standard the brief recites -- a reasonable belief that force is necessary to prevent death or great bodily injury to oneself or a third person. That formulation comes from the self-defense statute (OCGA § 16-3-21), which the brief also cites, not from Prayor. The citation thus partially supports the proposition: the forcible-felony limit is in the case, but the specific deadly-force-in-self-defense standard is not.

proposition: Deadly force is justified if the individual reasonably believes it is necessary to prevent death or great bodily injury to themselves or a third person, or to prevent a forcible felony.
brief_sentence: Specifically, deadly force is justified if the individual reasonably believes it is necessary to prevent death or great bodily injury to themselves or a third person, or to prevent a forcible felony. Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56, OCGA § 16-3-21, Chambers v. State, 308 Ga. App. 748.
brief_block: Specifically, deadly force is justified if the individual reasonably believes it is necessary to prevent death or great bodily injury to themselves or a third person, or to prevent a forcible felony. Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56, OCGA § 16-3-21, Chambers v. State, 308 Ga. App. 748.
opinion_block: [A] person is justified in using force which is intended or likely to cause death or great bodily harm only if he or she reasonably believes that such force is necessary to prevent death or great bodily injury to himself or herself or a third person or to prevent the commission of a forcible felony.
finding_analysis: Chambers quotes OCGA § 16-3-21 (a) and applies it as the controlling justification standard, including the precise rule that force intended or likely to cause death or great bodily harm is justified only on a reasonable belief that it is necessary to prevent death or great bodily injury to oneself or a third person, or to prevent a forcible felony. That statutory language tracks the brief's proposition almost word for word, so the cited case supports the stated principle.

proposition: Excessive or unlawful force, even when acting in self-defense, is not justifiable.
brief_sentence: Excessive or unlawful force, even when acting in self-defense, is not justifiable. Reddick v. State, 321 Ga. 73 (2025).
brief_block: Excessive or unlawful force, even when acting in self-defense, is not justifiable. Reddick v. State, 321 Ga. 73 (2025).
opinion_block: “The use of excessive force or unlawful force while acting in self-defense is not justifiable.”
finding_analysis: Reddick v. State directly supports this proposition. In its self-defense sufficiency analysis the opinion states (quoting Clark v. State, 271 Ga. 27, 29) that “[t]he use of excessive force or unlawful force while acting in self-defense is not justifiable,” and adds that deadly force “is not justified if the degree of force used by the defendant exceeds that which a reasonable person would believe necessary to defend against the victim's unlawful actions.” The brief's sentence is a faithful restatement of that rule.

proposition: The use of unreasonable force could not be part of a legitimate citizen's arrest, and such actions may lead to criminal liability.
brief_sentence: In Patel v. State, 279 Ga. 750, the Court emphasized that the use of unreasonable force could not be part of a legitimate citizen's arrest, and such actions may lead to criminal liability.
crosscheck_flags: {"toa_mismatch": {"variants": ["Patel v. State, 279 Ga. 750", "Patel v. State, 279 Ga. 50"]}}
brief_block: In Patel v. State, 279 Ga. 750, the Court emphasized that the use of unreasonable force could not be part of a legitimate citizen's arrest, and such actions may lead to criminal liability.
opinion_block: “Although a private person may make a citizen’s arrest under OCGA § 17-4-60, only force that is reasonable under the circumstances may be used to restrain the individual arrested ... the use of unreasonable force . . . could not have been part of a legitimate citizen’s arrest.”
finding_analysis:
Patel v. State directly states the first half of the proposition: quoting Carter v. State, the court recites that “only force that is reasonable under the circumstances may be used to restrain the individual arrested” and that “the use of unreasonable force . . . could not have been part of a legitimate citizen’s arrest.” To that extent the citation is accurate.

The added clause — that such actions “may lead to criminal liability” — is not a holding of the case. Patel is an ineffective-assistance-of-counsel decision in a felony-murder appeal; it discusses the unreasonable-force principle only to explain why the evidence did not warrant a jury charge on citizen's arrest, not to announce a rule about criminal liability flowing from excessive force. The brief extends the holding modestly beyond what the case decided.

proposition: A citizen's arrest charge is not warranted where the defendant did not personally witness the crime.
cited_for: No citizen's arrest instruction required where defendant did not personally witness the crime.
brief_sentence: A citizen's arrest charge is not warranted unless there is some evidence to support each statutory element. See Nelson v. State, 283 Ga. 119, 121 (2008) (no citizen's arrest instruction required where defendant did not personally witness the crime).
brief_block: A citizen's arrest charge is not warranted unless there is some evidence to support each statutory element. See Nelson v. State, 283 Ga. 119, 121 (2008) (no citizen's arrest instruction required where defendant did not personally witness the crime).
finding_analysis: Nelson v. State is a Georgia Supreme Court felony murder case about a fatal shooting outside a Valdosta bar and the defendant's justification defense. It does not address citizen's arrest. The opinion's jury-instruction discussion concerns only whether the defendant was entitled to charges on voluntary manslaughter, involuntary manslaughter, and reckless conduct as part of an ineffective-assistance claim; the page cited (121) is the start of the ineffective-assistance division. Nothing in the opinion mentions citizen's arrest, its statutory elements, or any requirement that a defendant personally witness a crime. The proposition attributed to this case does not appear in it.

proposition: The use of deadly force in effectuating a citizen's arrest must be reasonable and necessary under the circumstances.
brief_sentence: Moreover, the use of deadly force in effectuating a citizen's arrest must be reasonable and necessary under the circumstances. See OCGA § 17-4-20(b); Davis v. State, 269 Ga. 276, 277 (1998).
crosscheck_flags: {"toa_mismatch": {"variants": ["Davis v. State, 285 Ga. 343 (2009)", "Davis v. State, 269 Ga. 276, 277 (1998)"]}}
brief_block: Moreover, the use of deadly force in effectuating a citizen's arrest must be reasonable and necessary under the circumstances. See OCGA § 17-4-20(b); Davis v. State, 269 Ga. 276, 277 (1998).
finding_analysis: Davis v. State, 269 Ga. 276 (1998), is a felony-murder appeal about the stabbing death of Danny Reid. It does not address the use of deadly force in effecting a citizen's arrest, OCGA § 17-4-20(b), or any reasonableness-and-necessity standard for arrest force. The opinion's four divisions cover sufficiency of the evidence for felony murder, the admissibility of a first-offender guilty plea as similar-transaction evidence, the trial court's refusal to give a requested accident instruction, and Edge/Russell sequential-charge instructions. Nothing in the text touches citizen's arrest or deadly force in arrest. The proposition is attributed to a case that contains no such principle.

proposition: Trial counsel was not deficient for failing to request an instruction not adjusted to the evidence.
cited_for: No ineffective assistance for failing to request a charge not adjusted to the evidence.
brief_sentence: Thus, trial counsel was not deficient for failing to request an unsupported instruction. See Hamm v. State, 294 Ga. 791, 795 (2014) (no ineffective assistance for failing to request a charge not adjusted to the evidence).
brief_block: Thus, trial counsel was not deficient for failing to request an unsupported instruction. See Hamm v. State, 294 Ga. 791, 795 (2014) (no ineffective assistance for failing to request a charge not adjusted to the evidence).
finding_analysis: Hamm v. State is a direct-appeal murder case about whether the trial court erred in declining to give a requested jury instruction on accomplice corroboration. It does not address ineffective assistance of counsel. The opinion contains no discussion of deficient performance, Strickland, or trial counsel's conduct at all; it analyzes only whether the requested charge was authorized by "slight evidence" and concludes the trial court erred in refusing it but that the error was harmless. The brief cites the case for an ineffective-assistance principle -- that counsel is not deficient for failing to request a charge not adjusted to the evidence -- that appears nowhere in the opinion. The pincite (page 795) falls within the court's discussion of why refusing the instruction was error, the opposite posture from a deference-to-counsel holding.

proposition: A citizen's arrest requires the use of no more force than is reasonable under the circumstances, with deadly force limited to self-defense or preventing a forcible felony, and force is not justified if the individual was the initial aggressor unless they withdraw and effectively communicate their intent to do so.
brief_sentence: A citizen's arrest under OCGA § 17-4-60 requires that the arresting individual use no more force than is reasonable under the circumstances, and deadly force is limited to self-defense or preventing a forcible felony Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56, Hayes v. State, 261 Ga. 439.
brief_block: A citizen's arrest under OCGA § 17-4-60 requires that the arresting individual use no more force than is reasonable under the circumstances, and deadly force is limited to self-defense or preventing a forcible felony[.] Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56, Hayes v. State, 261 Ga. 439.
opinion_block: For a citizen's arrest to be valid, the citizen must use no more force than is reasonable under the circumstances. . . . Also, . . . deadly force in effecting an arrest is limited to self-defense or to a situation in which it is necessary to prevent a forcible felony.
finding_analysis: The first two components of this proposition -- no more force than reasonable, and deadly force limited to self-defense or preventing a forcible felony -- are squarely supported by the Hayes rule Prayor quotes. The third component, however, is not in the opinion: Prayor says nothing about an 'initial aggressor' losing justification unless he withdraws and effectively communicates his intent. That aggressor/withdrawal doctrine appears nowhere in this case. The citation supports the reasonable-force and deadly-force portions but overstates by attributing the aggressor rule to Prayor.

proposition: A citizen's arrest requires the use of no more force than is reasonable under the circumstances, with deadly force limited to self-defense or preventing a forcible felony, and force is not justified if the individual was the initial aggressor unless they withdraw and effectively communicate their intent to do so.
brief_sentence: A citizen's arrest under OCGA § 17-4-60 requires that the arresting individual use no more force than is reasonable under the circumstances, and deadly force is limited to self-defense or preventing a forcible felony Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56, Hayes v. State, 261 Ga. 439.
brief_block: A citizen's arrest under OCGA § 17-4-60 requires that the arresting individual use no more force than is reasonable under the circumstances, and deadly force is limited to self-defense or preventing a forcible felony Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56, Hayes v. State, 261 Ga. 439.
opinion_block: OCGA § 17-4-60 provides that a private citizen may make an arrest if a felony is committed in his presence or within his immediate knowledge. ... For a citizen's arrest to be valid, the citizen must use no more force than is reasonable under the circumstances. ... [D]eadly force in effecting an arrest is limited to self-defense or to a situation in which it is necessary to prevent a forcible felony. OCGA § 17-4-20.
finding_analysis: The first half of this proposition is well supported: the opinion expressly states, in connection with OCGA § 17-4-60, that a valid citizen's arrest requires no more force than is reasonable under the circumstances and that deadly force is limited to self-defense or preventing a forcible felony. The second half of the proposition, however -- that force is not justified if the individual was the initial aggressor unless they withdraw and effectively communicate their intent to do so -- does not appear in this opinion. Hayes does not address initial-aggressor or withdrawal doctrine at all; that principle is added by the brief and is not attributable to this case. Because the proposition extends beyond what the opinion decided, it is only partially supported.

proposition: The use of force is not justified if the individual was the initial aggressor, unless they withdraw from the encounter and effectively communicate their intent to do so and the other party continues or threatens to continue the use of unlawful force.
brief_sentence: Additionally, the use of force is not justified if the individual was the initial aggressor, unless they withdraw from the encounter and effectively communicate their intent to do so, and the other party continues or threatens to continue the use of unlawful force OCGA § 16-3-21, Wainwright v. State, 305 Ga. 63 (2019), Powers v. State, 297 Ga. 345 (2015).
brief_block: Additionally, the use of force is not justified if the individual was the initial aggressor, unless they withdraw from the encounter and effectively communicate their intent to do so, and the other party continues or threatens to continue the use of unlawful force OCGA § 16-3-21, Wainwright v. State, 305 Ga. 63 (2019), Powers v. State, 297 Ga. 345 (2015).
opinion_block: (3) Was the aggressor or was engaged in combat by agreement unless he withdraws from the encounter and effectively communicates to such other person his intent to do so and the other, notwithstanding, continues or threatens to continue the use of unlawful force.
finding_analysis: Wainwright v. State quotes OCGA § 16-3-21 (b)(3) verbatim and relies on it to deny the justification charge, so the brief's proposition that an initial aggressor is not justified unless he withdraws, effectively communicates his intent to do so, and the other party continues or threatens to continue unlawful force tracks the rule the opinion actually applies. The brief's language is a close paraphrase of the statutory text the opinion sets out, and the case is correctly cited for that aggressor-withdrawal rule.

proposition: The use of force is not justified if the individual was the initial aggressor, unless they withdraw from the encounter and effectively communicate their intent to do so and the other party continues or threatens to continue the use of unlawful force.
brief_sentence: Additionally, the use of force is not justified if the individual was the initial aggressor, unless they withdraw from the encounter and effectively communicate their intent to do so, and the other party continues or threatens to continue the use of unlawful force OCGA § 16-3-21, Wainwright v. State, 305 Ga. 63 (2019), Powers v. State, 297 Ga. 345 (2015).
brief_block: Additionally, the use of force is not justified if the individual was the initial aggressor, unless they withdraw from the encounter and effectively communicate their intent to do so, and the other party continues or threatens to continue the use of unlawful force OCGA § 16-3-21, Wainwright v. State, 305 Ga. 63 (2019), Powers v. State, 297 Ga. 345 (2015).
opinion_block: And a defendant is not entitled to a jury instruction on justification when the evidence is that the supposedly justified party was the aggressor. See OCGA § 16-3-21 (b) (3) (“A person is not justified in using force . . . if he . . . [w]as the aggressor ... unless he withdraws from the encounter and effectively communicates to such other person his intent to do so and the other, notwithstanding, continues or threatens to continue the use of unlawful force.”)
finding_analysis: Powers v. State supports the proposition. The opinion expressly quotes and applies OCGA § 16-3-21 (b) (3), holding that “a defendant is not entitled to a jury instruction on justification when the evidence is that the supposedly justified party was the aggressor,” and reciting the statute's withdrawal-and-communication exception in language that tracks the brief's proposition.

proposition: A jury charge on citizen's arrest was not warranted because there was no evidence that the defendant was attempting to effectuate an arrest and the defendant used more force than was reasonable under the circumstances.
brief_sentence: The Court held in Patel v. State, 279 Ga. 50, that a jury charge on citizen's arrest was not warranted because there was no evidence that the defendant was attempting to effectuate an arrest, and the defendant used more force than was reasonable under the circumstances.

proposition: A defendant is not entitled to a jury instruction on justification, including in the context of a citizen's arrest, when the evidence shows that the defendant was the aggressor.
brief_sentence: Similarly, in Wainwright v. State, 305 Ga. 63, the Court determined that a defendant is not entitled to a jury instruction on justification, including in the context of a citizen's arrest, when the evidence shows that the defendant was the aggressor.
brief_block: Similarly, in Wainwright v. State, 305 Ga. 63, the Court determined that a defendant is not entitled to a jury instruction on justification, including in the context of a citizen's arrest, when the evidence shows that the defendant was the aggressor.
opinion_block: Here, the evidence shows Wainwright initiated the assault and robbery of the victims by aggressively approaching Arnold and pointing a gun in his face before demanding Sanusi to empty his pockets at gunpoint. Under these circumstances, Wainwright was not entitled to a jury charge on justification.
finding_analysis:
The core of the proposition is supported: Wainwright holds that because the defendant initiated the assault and robbery, he was not entitled to a jury charge on justification, quoting Powers for the rule that a defendant is not entitled to a justification instruction when the evidence is that the supposedly justified party was the aggressor.

The overstatement is the qualifier "including in the context of a citizen's arrest." The Wainwright opinion does not mention citizen's arrest anywhere; its justification analysis concerns self-defense and defense of others under OCGA § 16-3-21, not citizen's arrest. The aggressor-no-justification holding is accurate, but the brief extends it to a citizen's-arrest context the case never addresses.

proposition: A jury charge on a defendant's sole defense is only required if there is some evidence to support the charge, and even slight evidence is sufficient, but there must be a legitimate basis for the defense.
quoted_text: ["but there must be a legitimate basis for the defense."]
brief_sentence: Even slight evidence is sufficient to warrant such a charge, but there must be a legitimate basis for the defense. (Emphasis added) Tarvestad v. State, 261 Ga. 605, Virgil v. State, 227 Ga. App. 96.
quote_check: [{"quote": "but there must be a legitimate basis for the defense.", "result": "FABRICATED", "similarity": 0.47, "matched_passage": "at a review of all of the evidence showed that Tarvestad had options other than driving the truck. The trial court must charge the jury on the defendant\u2019s sole defense, even without a written request, if there is some evidence to support t"}]
brief_block: Even slight evidence is sufficient to warrant such a charge, but there must be a legitimate basis for the defense. (Emphasis added) Tarvestad v. State, 261 Ga. 605, Virgil v. State, 227 Ga. App. 96.
opinion_block: The trial court must charge the jury on the defendant's sole defense, even without a written request, if there is some evidence to support the charge.
finding_analysis:
Tarvestad squarely supports the core of the proposition: the opinion holds that the trial court must charge the jury on the defendant's sole defense, even without a written request, if there is some evidence to support the charge. That "some evidence" standard is the holding's centerpiece.

The brief, however, layers on two qualifiers the opinion does not state. Tarvestad contains no "even slight evidence is sufficient" formulation, and it nowhere requires that "there must be a legitimate basis for the defense" -- the quoted phrase does not appear in the opinion (consistent with the FABRICATED quote-check result). Those glosses may come from the companion citation, but as attributed to Tarvestad they overstate what this case decided, so the support is partial rather than full.

proposition: A jury charge on a defendant's sole defense is only required if there is some evidence to support the charge, and even slight evidence is sufficient, but there must be a legitimate basis for the defense.
quoted_text: ["but there must be a legitimate basis for the defense."]
brief_sentence: Even slight evidence is sufficient to warrant such a charge, but there must be a legitimate basis for the defense. (Emphasis added) Tarvestad v. State, 261 Ga. 605, Virgil v. State, 227 Ga. App. 96.
quote_check: [{"quote": "but there must be a legitimate basis for the defense.", "result": "FABRICATED", "similarity": 0.5, "matched_passage": "2. As to Virgil\u2019s contention that the trial court erred by refusing to give her requested charge on the defense of accident, we find no basis for a charge on the defense of accident under th"}]
brief_block: Even slight evidence is sufficient to warrant such a charge, but there must be a legitimate basis for the defense. (Emphasis added) Tarvestad v. State, 261 Ga. 605, Virgil v. State, 227 Ga. App. 96.
opinion_block: As to Virgil's contention that the trial court erred by refusing to give her requested charge on the defense of accident, we find no basis for a charge on the defense of accident under the facts of this case.
finding_analysis:
Virgil v. State is a DUI case in which the defendant argued the trial court erred by refusing to charge the jury on the defense of accident. The opinion supports the general notion that a charge on a defense requires a legitimate basis in the facts -- the court found "no basis for a charge on the defense of accident under the facts of this case" because Virgil did not actually claim any contested element of the offense was done by accident, but only that she was not driving or in actual physical control of the car.

The opinion does not, however, articulate the brief's specific standard that "even slight evidence is sufficient" to warrant such a charge. The quoted phrase "but there must be a legitimate basis for the defense" does not appear in the opinion (the quote check flags it as FABRICATED). The case is thus topically aligned with the legitimate-basis half of the proposition but does not state the slight-evidence rule the brief attributes to it, so the support is partial rather than full.

proposition: A citizen who uses excessive force loses the protection of the citizen's arrest statute and cannot rely on the statute as a defense against criminal or civil claims.
brief_sentence: A citizen who uses excessive force loses the protection of the citizen's arrest statute. This means that the arrest is no longer considered lawful, and the individual cannot rely on the statute as a defense against criminal or civil claims. Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56, Patel v. State, 279 Ga. 750.
brief_block: A citizen who uses excessive force loses the protection of the citizen's arrest statute. This means that the arrest is no longer considered lawful, and the individual cannot rely on the statute as a defense against criminal or civil claims. Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56, Patel v. State, 279 Ga. 750.
opinion_block: under the evidence adduced at trial the jury was authorized to reject his citizen's arrest/justification and self-defense/justification defenses and to convict him of the crime charged.
finding_analysis: Prayor supports the general thrust of this proposition but not its specific framing. The opinion establishes that a valid citizen's arrest permits no more than reasonable force, and it holds that the jury was authorized to reject the defendant's citizen's-arrest justification defense and convict him -- which is consistent with the idea that excessive force defeats the defense. But the opinion does not state the principle that excessive force 'loses the protection of the statute,' and it is a criminal aggravated-assault appeal that says nothing about civil claims. The citation reasonably supports the criminal-defense point in a general way but overstates by adding the loss-of-statutory-protection framing and the civil-claims extension, neither of which appears in Prayor.

proposition: A citizen who uses excessive force loses the protection of the citizen's arrest statute and cannot rely on the statute as a defense against criminal or civil claims.
brief_sentence: A citizen who uses excessive force loses the protection of the citizen's arrest statute. This means that the arrest is no longer considered lawful, and the individual cannot rely on the statute as a defense against criminal or civil claims. Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56, Patel v. State, 279 Ga. 750.
crosscheck_flags: {"toa_mismatch": {"variants": ["Patel v. State, 279 Ga. 750", "Patel v. State, 279 Ga. 50"]}}
brief_block: A citizen who uses excessive force loses the protection of the citizen's arrest statute. This means that the arrest is no longer considered lawful, and the individual cannot rely on the statute as a defense against criminal or civil claims. [...] Patel v. State, 279 Ga. 750.
opinion_block: “... the use of unreasonable force . . . could not have been part of a legitimate citizen’s arrest.” Here, Patel did not attempt to restrain the victim; rather, he shot and killed him minutes after commanding him to halt.
finding_analysis:
Patel v. State supports the core idea that unreasonable force takes conduct outside the protection of the citizen's-arrest statute: it quotes Carter v. State that “the use of unreasonable force . . . could not have been part of a legitimate citizen’s arrest” and applies that to hold that Patel’s shooting could not be a lawful citizen's arrest.

The brief overstates by framing the case as a rule that the citizen “cannot rely on the statute as a defense against criminal or civil claims.” Patel is a criminal felony-murder/ineffective-assistance decision; it holds only that the evidence did not support a citizen's-arrest jury charge, and it says nothing about civil claims. The proposition is topically grounded but extends the holding to a defense-availability rule the opinion does not articulate.

proposition: Deadly force in effecting an arrest is limited to self-defense or preventing a forcible felony, and any use of force beyond what is reasonable invalidates the arrest.
brief_sentence: The Court reiterated in Prayor that deadly force in effecting an arrest is limited to self-defense or preventing a forcible felony, and any use of force beyond what is reasonable invalidates the arrest. Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56.
brief_block: The Court reiterated in Prayor that deadly force in effecting an arrest is limited to self-defense or preventing a forcible felony, and any use of force beyond what is reasonable invalidates the arrest. Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56.
opinion_block: For a citizen's arrest to be valid, the citizen must use no more force than is reasonable under the circumstances. . . . Also, . . . deadly force in effecting an arrest is limited to self-defense or to a situation in which it is necessary to prevent a forcible felony.
finding_analysis: The deadly-force half of this proposition is fully supported: Prayor (quoting Hayes) states that deadly force in effecting an arrest is limited to self-defense or to preventing a forcible felony. The second clause overstates slightly. The opinion says a valid citizen's arrest requires 'no more force than is reasonable under the circumstances,' but it does not frame excess force as something that 'invalidates the arrest.' That is a reasonable but unstated extension of the opinion's reasonable-force requirement, so the citation partially supports the proposition.

proposition: Deadly force is strictly limited to lawful self-defense or prevention of a forcible felony, and its use is always subject to judicial scrutiny and jury determination.
brief_sentence: Deadly force is strictly limited to lawful self-defense or prevention of a forcible felony, and its use is always subject to judicial scrutiny and jury determination. Johnson v. Jackson, 140 Ga. App. 252; Merneigh v. State, 242 Ga. App. 735; Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56; OCGA § 16-3-21.
brief_block: Deadly force is strictly limited to lawful self-defense or prevention of a forcible felony, and its use is always subject to judicial scrutiny and jury determination. Johnson v. Jackson, 140 Ga. App. 252 [...].
opinion_block: The use of force which is intended or likely to cause death or great bodily harm to prevent trespass on or other tortious or criminal interference with real property (other than a habitation) or personal property is not justified unless the person using such force reasonably believes that it is necessary to prevent the commission of a forcible felony.
finding_analysis: Johnson v. Jackson supports the core of this proposition but the brief overstates it. The opinion ties the use of deadly force to prevention of a forcible felony (quoting Criminal Code Sec. 26-904(b)) and repeatedly holds that whether the force used was reasonable and whether the killing was necessary are questions for the jury, so the jury-determination point is squarely grounded. But the case does not state, as a categorical rule, that deadly force is "strictly limited to lawful self-defense or prevention of a forcible felony"; self-defense is not a focus, and the opinion also recognizes a citizen's authority to use force in effecting a felony arrest. The proposition is topically on point and the holding extends to most of it, but the "strictly limited to lawful self-defense" framing reaches beyond what the case decided.

proposition: Deadly force is strictly limited to lawful self-defense or prevention of a forcible felony, and its use is always subject to judicial scrutiny and jury determination.
brief_sentence: Deadly force is strictly limited to lawful self-defense or prevention of a forcible felony, and its use is always subject to judicial scrutiny and jury determination. Johnson v. Jackson, 140 Ga. App. 252; Merneigh v. State, 242 Ga. App. 735; Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56; OCGA § 16-3-21.
brief_block: Deadly force is strictly limited to lawful self-defense or prevention of a forcible felony, and its use is always subject to judicial scrutiny and jury determination. Johnson v. Jackson, 140 Ga. App. 252; Merneigh v. State, 242 Ga. App. 735; Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56; OCGA § 16-3-21.
finding_analysis:
Merneigh v. State is a criminal appeal about theft by shoplifting and aggravated assault, addressing the sufficiency of an indictment, jury instructions, citizen's-arrest authority under OCGA § 17-4-60, a lesser-included reckless-conduct charge, juror strikes for cause, and use of an alias in the indictment. It does not address the proposition that deadly force is strictly limited to lawful self-defense or prevention of a forcible felony, nor that its use is always subject to judicial scrutiny and jury determination.

The opinion's only brush with self-defense is Division 5, where the court held the trial court properly refused justification and self-defense charges because no evidence supported them -- a record-specific ruling, not a statement of any limit on deadly force. Nothing in the opinion announces the cited principle, so the case does not support the proposition.

proposition: Deadly force is strictly limited to lawful self-defense or prevention of a forcible felony, and its use is always subject to judicial scrutiny and jury determination.
brief_sentence: Deadly force is strictly limited to lawful self-defense or prevention of a forcible felony, and its use is always subject to judicial scrutiny and jury determination. Johnson v. Jackson, 140 Ga. App. 252; Merneigh v. State, 242 Ga. App. 735; Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56; OCGA § 16-3-21.
brief_block: Deadly force is strictly limited to lawful self-defense or prevention of a forcible felony, and its use is always subject to judicial scrutiny and jury determination. Johnson v. Jackson, 140 Ga. App. 252; Merneigh v. State, 242 Ga. App. 735; Prayor v. State, 217 Ga. App. 56; OCGA § 16-3-21.
opinion_block: deadly force in effecting an arrest is limited to self-defense or to a situation in which it is necessary to prevent a forcible felony.
finding_analysis: The core deadly-force limitation -- confined to self-defense or prevention of a forcible felony -- is directly supported by Prayor's quotation of the Hayes rule. The added assertion that deadly force is 'always subject to judicial scrutiny and jury determination' is only loosely grounded: Prayor does illustrate a jury rejecting the citizen's-arrest and self-defense justifications and convicting (section 2), but the opinion does not announce any general rule that deadly force is 'always' subject to judicial scrutiny and jury determination. The citation supports the deadly-force limit and is consistent with jury determination on the facts, but the categorical 'always' framing extends beyond what the opinion holds, making this partial.

proposition: When a defendant is the initial aggressor and the evidence does not support the statutory elements of a lawful citizen's arrest, the trial court is not required to instruct the jury on citizen's arrest.
brief_sentence: Further, when a defendant is the initial aggressor and the evidence does not support the statutory elements of a lawful citizen's arrest, the trial court is not required to instruct the jury on citizen's arrest. Wainwright v. State, 305 Ga. 63; Patel v. State, 279 Ga. 750; Virgil v. State, 227 Ga. App. 96.
brief_block: Further, when a defendant is the initial aggressor and the evidence does not support the statutory elements of a lawful citizen's arrest, the trial court is not required to instruct the jury on citizen's arrest. Wainwright v. State, 305 Ga. 63; Patel v. State, 279 Ga. 750; Virgil v. State, 227 Ga. App. 96.
finding_analysis:
Wainwright v. State does not address citizen's arrest at all. The opinion's justification discussion (Division 5(b)) is confined to self-defense and defense of others under OCGA § 16-3-21; it never mentions a citizen's-arrest instruction, the statutory elements of a lawful citizen's arrest, or any rule about when such an instruction is required.

The proposition attributes a citizen's-arrest-specific holding -- that no citizen's-arrest charge is required where the aggressor's evidence does not satisfy the statutory elements -- to a case that contains no such principle. The shared "initial aggressor" vocabulary does not bridge the gap, because the brief's proposition turns on a distinct legal issue (the citizen's-arrest instruction and its elements) that Wainwright simply does not decide.

proposition: When a defendant is the initial aggressor and the evidence does not support the statutory elements of a lawful citizen's arrest, the trial court is not required to instruct the jury on citizen's arrest.
brief_sentence: Further, when a defendant is the initial aggressor and the evidence does not support the statutory elements of a lawful citizen's arrest, the trial court is not required to instruct the jury on citizen's arrest. Wainwright v. State, 305 Ga. 63; Patel v. State, 279 Ga. 750; Virgil v. State, 227 Ga. App. 96.
crosscheck_flags: {"toa_mismatch": {"variants": ["Patel v. State, 279 Ga. 750", "Patel v. State, 279 Ga. 50"]}}
brief_block: Further, when a defendant is the initial aggressor and the evidence does not support the statutory elements of a lawful citizen's arrest, the trial court is not required to instruct the jury on citizen's arrest. [...] Patel v. State, 279 Ga. 750 [...].
opinion_block: Here, Patel did not attempt to restrain the victim; rather, he shot and killed him minutes after commanding him to halt. As in Carter, supra, there was no evidentiary support in this case to warrant a charge on OCGA § 17-4-60. Thus, trial counsel was not ineffective in failing to request such a charge.
finding_analysis:
Patel v. State supports the operative principle that when the evidence does not support the statutory elements of a citizen's arrest, no jury charge on OCGA § 17-4-60 is required: the court found “no evidentiary support in this case to warrant a charge” because Patel shot the victim rather than attempting to restrain him.

The brief overstates by attributing the result to the defendant being “the initial aggressor.” That is not the court’s rationale; the holding rests on the absence of any attempt to restrain (and that “any assault . . . was over when [Patel] started shooting”), not on an initial-aggressor theory. The no-instruction-when-elements-unsupported point is squarely supported, but the “initial aggressor” framing recharacterizes the basis of the decision.

finding_analysis:
Virgil v. State is a DUI case about whether the trial court should have charged the jury on the defense of accident and whether an "actual physical control" instruction was harmful error. It does not address citizen's arrest, an initial aggressor, the statutory elements of a lawful citizen's arrest, or any jury instruction on citizen's arrest.

The opinion's discussion of refusing a defense charge is confined entirely to the defense of accident in a drunk-driving prosecution; nothing in it concerns the citizen's-arrest principle the brief cites it for. The citation does not support the proposition.

proposition: The standard for reviewing sufficiency of the evidence is whether the evidence was sufficient for a rational trier of fact to find beyond a reasonable doubt that the defendant was guilty of the charged offenses.
brief_sentence: The standard for reviewing the sufficiency of evidence is whether the evidence was sufficient for a rational trier of fact to find beyond a reasonable doubt that the defendant was guilty of the charged offenses. Jackson v. Virginia, 443 U.S. 307 (1979).

Jackson, 140 Ga. App. 252 (1976), a Georgia Court of Appeals civil case about a landowner's citizen's arrest and use of deadly force against a fleeing burglary suspect. The provided opinion does not contain the rational-trier-of-fact sufficiency standard and is a different case entirely, so this proposition cannot be supported from the opinion before me.

proposition: In determining evidentiary sufficiency, the evidence is viewed in the light most favorable to the verdict, and the court will not weigh the evidence or determine witness credibility.
brief_sentence: In determining evidentiary sufficiency, the evidence is viewed in the light most favorable to the verdict, and the court will not weigh the evidence or determine witness credibility. Caldwell v. State, 263 Ga. 560, 436 S.E.2d 488 (1993); see also Vega v. State, 285 Ga. 32, 673 S.E.2d 223 (2009).
brief_block: In determining evidentiary sufficiency, the evidence is viewed in the light most favorable to the verdict, and the court will not weigh the evidence or determine witness credibility. Caldwell v. State, 263 Ga. 560, 436 S.E.2d 488 (1993) [...]
opinion_block: This court does not weigh the evidence on appeal or resolve conflicts in trial testimony. Rather, it is the function of this court to examine the evidence in the light most favorable to the verdict and to determine whether any rational trier of fact could have found appellant guilty beyond a reasonable doubt.
finding_analysis: Caldwell v. State directly supports the proposition. In Division 1, the opinion states the standard of review for evidentiary sufficiency: the court does not weigh the evidence or resolve conflicts in testimony, but examines the evidence in the light most favorable to the verdict to determine whether any rational trier of fact could have found guilt beyond a reasonable doubt (citing Jackson v. Virginia). That language matches the brief's assertion that the evidence is viewed in the light most favorable to the verdict and that the court will not weigh evidence or determine witness credibility.
