proposition: If there is some competent evidence, even though contradicted, to support each fact necessary to make out the State's case, the jury's verdict will be upheld.
brief_sentence: If there is some competent evidence, even though contradicted, to support each fact necessary to make out the State's case, the jury's verdict will be upheld. Santiago v. State, 314 Ga. App. 623, 623, 724 S.E.2d 793, 794 (2012).
brief_block: If there is some competent evidence, even though contradicted, to support each fact necessary to make out the State's case, the jury's verdict will be upheld. Santiago v. State, 314 Ga. App. 623, 623, 724 S.E.2d 793, 794 (2012).
opinion_block: As long as there is some competent evidence, even though contradicted, to support each fact necessary to make out the state's case, the jury's verdict will be upheld.
finding_analysis: Santiago v. State recites this exact standard of appellate review for sufficiency of the evidence in a quoted passage near the opening of the opinion, and the brief's sentence tracks that language almost verbatim. The proposition is directly and accurately supported.

proposition: Whether a witness is credible and whether his testimony should be accepted are questions for a jury.
brief_sentence: Whether a witness is credible and whether his testimony should be accepted are questions for a jury. See Goldsby v. State, 273 Ga. App. 523 (2005).
brief_block: Whether a witness is credible and whether his testimony should be accepted are questions for a jury. See Goldsby v. State, 273 Ga. App. 523 (2005).
opinion_block: Whether the witness is credible and whether her [testimony] should be accepted are questions for the jury.
finding_analysis: Goldsby v. State directly supports the proposition. In rejecting Richard Goldsby's challenge to a witness's identification, the court held that the argument "go[es] to the weight and credit to be given the evidence by the trier of fact and not to the sufficiency of the evidence. Whether the witness is credible and whether her [testimony] should be accepted are questions for the jury," adding that "[i]t is not the role of the appellate court to evaluate witness credibility." The brief's phrasing tracks this holding almost verbatim, and the gender swap (his/her) reflects only that the witness in Goldsby was female.

proposition: The sufficiency of the evidence, including any conflicts, the credibility of witnesses, and the weight of the evidence, supports the verdict where the verdict does not offend the principles of justice and equity.
brief_sentence: The sufficiency of the evidence, including any conflicts in the evidence, the credibility of the witnesses, and the weight of the evidence, supports the verdict and the verdict does not offend the principles of justice and/or equity. Choisnet v. State, 292 Ga. 860, 861, 742 S.E.2d 476 (2013).
brief_block: The sufficiency of the evidence, including any conflicts in the evidence, the credibility of the witnesses, and the weight of the evidence, supports the verdict and the verdict does not offend the principles of justice and/or equity. Choisnet v. State, 292 Ga. 860, 861, 742 S.E.2d 476 (2013).
opinion_block: However, a trial court does not fulfill its duty to exercise its discretion when it applies the standard of review set out in Jackson v. Virginia to the statutory grounds for a new trial.... Inasmuch as only the trial court is authorized by law to review a verdict pursuant to OCGA §§ 5-5-20 and 5-5-21, we agree with the parties that the judgment must be vacated and the case remanded to the trial court for consideration of the amended motion for new trial under the proper legal standard.
finding_analysis:
Choisnet v. State does not support the brief's proposition. The opinion is a procedural decision that vacated the conviction and remanded the case because the trial court applied the wrong legal standard to the motion for new trial, using the Jackson v. Virginia sufficiency-of-the-evidence test instead of independently weighing the evidence and assessing witness credibility as a "thirteenth juror" under OCGA §§ 5-5-20 and 5-5-21.

The brief recasts this as an affirmative holding that the evidence "supports the verdict" and that the verdict does not offend the principles of justice and equity. The opinion holds the opposite of that framing: it found the trial court had not yet properly assessed the weight of the evidence or witness credibility at all, and it remanded precisely so that review could be done correctly. Although the opinion shares the brief's vocabulary (weight of the evidence, conflicts in testimony, credibility of witnesses, principles of justice), it makes no determination that the evidence supports the verdict, and its holding cannot reasonably be extended to support that proposition.

proposition: The evidence admitted at trial, including lay testimony, expert testimony, and exhibits, was sufficient for a rational trier of fact to find the defendant guilty of all charges beyond a reasonable doubt.
brief_sentence: The evidence admitted at trial, including all lay witness testimony, expert testimony, and exhibits, was sufficient for a rational trier of fact to find the Defendant guilty of all charges, beyond a reasonable doubt. Jackson v. Virginia, 443 U.S. 307 (1979).

Jackson, 140 Ga. App. 252 (1976), an unrelated Georgia citizen's-arrest and deadly-force case. The provided opinion contains no sufficiency-of-the-evidence standard and does not address this proposition; it is a different case than the one cited.

proposition: Reviewing courts do not reweigh evidence or resolve conflicts in testimony; instead, evidence is reviewed in the light most favorable to the verdict, with deference to the jury's assessment of the weight and credibility of the evidence.
brief_sentence: Reviewing courts do not reweigh evidence or resolve conflicts in testimony; instead, evidence is reviewed in the light most favorable to the verdict, with deference to the jury's assessment of the weight and credibility of the evidence. Smiley v. State, 300 Ga. 582, 583 (1) (2017) (quoting Mickens v. State, 277 Ga. 627, 627-628 (2004)).

finding_analysis: Smiley v. State supports the proposition. In its Division 1 sufficiency-of-the-evidence discussion (at page 583), the opinion states the standard nearly verbatim, holding that the court does not reweigh evidence or resolve conflicts in testimony and that evidence is reviewed in the light most favorable to the verdict with deference to the jury's assessment of weight and credibility.
